name: Wavelength: Sleep & Jet Lag
subtitle: Circadian rhythm light meter

screenshots.caption: Jet lag: land already on local time
screenshots.alt: Jet lag: this light is shifting your sleep earlier

screenshots.alt: Better sleep: keep it dim, screen near-black at night

screenshots.alt: Shift work: this light is shifting your sleep later

description:
Better sleep and easier jet lag start with light. Wavelength reads the light around you and coaches you on exactly when to seek it and when to avoid it, so your body clock lands where you want it. Better-aligned sleep is also linked in research to steadier mood and focus.

It measures melanopic light, the specific wavelengths that regulate your sleep-wake cycle. Unlike a standard light meter, it tells you whether your current lighting is helping or hurting your sleep goals.

HOW IT WORKS

Point your iPhone camera at the light around you. Wavelength analyzes the color temperature and intensity to estimate melanopic lux, the measure of light that drives your circadian system. You get an immediate, color-coded reading with a plain-language recommendation.

SMART RECOMMENDATIONS

Wavelength doesn't just show numbers. It factors in:
- Your current and target sleep schedule
- The time of day and your circadian phase
- Whether you're trying to shift your sleep earlier or later
- The phase response curve from circadian research

Based on all of this, it tells you whether to seek or avoid light right now, and when the next window opens.

DAILY NOTIFICATIONS

Set your current bedtime and your goal bedtime. Wavelength schedules daily reminders:
- When to start dimming lights before bed
- When bright light exposure will shift your sleep in the right direction
- When to avoid light that would work against your goals

BUILT ON SCIENCE

Wavelength implements recommendations from Brown et al. (2022) in PLOS Biology, the expert consensus on indoor light for healthy sleep:
- Daytime: 250+ melanopic lux for alertness
- Evening: Under 10 melanopic lux before bed
- Sleep: Under 1 melanopic lux

Phase and amplitude response curves are based on Hannay et al. (2019) in the Journal of Biological Rhythms.

DESIGNED FOR

- Anyone who wants to sleep better
- Travelers managing jet lag across time zones
- Shift workers with irregular schedules
- Athletes optimizing recovery
- People exploring light therapy for sleep

WHAT YOU'LL NEED

- iPhone with a camera
- That's it. No accessories, no special hardware.

Note: Camera-based light measurement is approximate (not a replacement for a laboratory spectrometer) but well-suited for the relative comparisons that matter for circadian health.
note:
This is a preview, not the live App Store listing.
Screenshots are the exact 6.7" PNGs from the app build, shown bare as Apple displays them. The second frame is the app's night mode: the whole UI fades to near-black before bed.
